But that never stopped me from understanding what's fair and what's not. When I look at this gender gap in science, I see something that bothers me, even if it's not directly in my field. My daughter asked me about studying engineering once, and I remember thinking how few women she'd see in those programs. That shouldn't be how it works.

Yes, absolutely we should address it. Here's why: we're losing half the potential talent in science because women face barriers that men simply don't encounter. I've worked alongside capable women in construction and technical fields, and they're just as skilled, just as dedicated. The problem isn't ability—it's opportunity and how systems are set up. When girls are discouraged from STEM subjects early on, when they see few role models in laboratories, when they face skepticism about their technical competence, we all lose.

How do we fix this? It starts young, in schools. We need to actively encourage girls to pursue science and mathematics without the biases that still exist. Teachers need training to recognize their own unconscious prejudices. Universities should examine their hiring and promotion practices honestly. I've seen discrimination in my own trade—fewer women get apprenticeships because people assume they can't handle the work. The same thing happens in science.

Companies and research institutions need to create genuine support systems: mentorship programs, flexible work arrangements that don't penalize ambition, and transparent career pathways. And we need to celebrate women scientists publicly so young girls see that this path is for them too. It's not about lowering standards or giving anyone a free pass. It's about removing the invisible obstacles that shouldn't be there.

My anxiety about change aside, I know this is necessary. A society that ignores half its potential minds is foolish. response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
Look, I've spent my whole life working with my hands as a plumber here in Bari, and I'll be honest with you—I didn't have the fancy education my mother had with her master's degree. But that never stopped me from understanding what's fair and what's not. When I look at this gender gap in science, I see something that bothers me, even if it's not directly in my field. My daughter asked me about studying engineering once, and I remember thinking how few women she'd see in those programs. That shouldn't be how it works.

Yes, absolutely we should address it. Here's why: we're losing half the potential talent in science because women face barriers that men simply don't encounter. I've worked alongside capable women in construction and technical fields, and they're just as skilled, just as dedicated. The problem isn't ability—it's opportunity and how systems are set up. When girls are discouraged from STEM subjects early on, when they see few role models in laboratories, when they face skepticism about their technical competence, we all lose.

How do we fix this? It starts young, in schools. We need to actively encourage girls to pursue science and mathematics without the biases that still exist. Teachers need training to recognize their own unconscious prejudices. Universities should examine their hiring and promotion practices honestly. I've seen discrimination in my own trade—fewer women get apprenticeships because people assume they can't handle the work. The same thing happens in science.

Companies and research institutions need to create genuine support systems: mentorship programs, flexible work arrangements that don't penalize ambition, and transparent career pathways. And we need to celebrate women scientists publicly so young girls see that this path is for them too. It's not about lowering standards or giving anyone a free pass. It's about removing the invisible obstacles that shouldn't be there.

My anxiety about change aside, I know this is necessary. A society that ignores half its potential minds is foolish. We need more women in science—not because of guilt or quotas, but because innovation and discovery benefit when we include everyone capable of contributing.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: This persona is a 66-year-old self-employed plumber from Bari with moderate openness but high conscientiousness and extraversion. His mixed educational background—vocational training like his father but with a mother holding a master's degree—creates awareness of educational disparities. His high conscientiousness suggests belief in fairness and systems that work properly. High extraversion makes him comfortable discussing social issues. His moderate agreeableness indicates he'll be direct rather than purely diplomatic. His anxiety suggests some caution about change, but his practical mindset and lived experience with skilled trades gives him credibility in discussing discrimination. He'd approach this from a pragmatic, fairness-based perspective rather than academic theory, drawing on his experience with gender dynamics in technical fields. His hobbies suggest someone thoughtful and detail-oriented, not dismissive of complex issues.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, practical, and earnest. A working-class perspective grounded in lived experience rather than academic language. Slightly anxious but ultimately persuasive, speaking from values of fairness and meritocracy. Warm and relatable, using personal examples to illustrate larger points.
citations: European Commission on Gender in Science
